| Baker's Ammonia | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Category | Leavening agents |

Cookbook | Recipes | Ingredients | Equipment | Techniques | Cookbook Disambiguation Pages | Ingredients

**Baker's ammonia**—sometimes referred to by the archaic names **horn salt** or **hartshorn**, the chemical name **ammonium (bi)carbonate**, or the nickname **vol**—is a leavening agent.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:8-6)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Like other chemical leaveners, the product is sold as an alkaline white powder that has a very strong odor of ammonia.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) Key to its leavening function is the ability of baker's ammonia to decompose readily above 40°C (104°F), producing water as well as gases of ammonia and carbon dioxide.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8)[[9]](#cite_note-:6-9) Critically, this leaves behind no chemical salt residues that could cause off-flavors in the baked product,[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) and it produces a large amount of gas and thereby leavening power.[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5) On the other hand, because the ammonia produced is water-soluble, it can leave behind an undesirable flavor and odor if insufficient water is driven off during baking.[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)[[10]](#cite_note-:9-10) Baker's ammonia does not require acid to decompose into gas,[[4]](#cite_note-:5-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:4-8) and it will not break down at typical room temperatures.[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) This last property makes doughs containing baker's ammonia generally bench rest-tolerant, but it is rapid-acting once it encounters heat.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Baker's ammonia can be hard to find from basic bakery suppliers,[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) but specialists make carry it. Make sure to store it in an airtight container away from light, heat, and moisture.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Because of its limitations, this leavener is used for a narrow range of baked goods as an alternative to (and sometimes improvement on) baking powder and soda.[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)[[9]](#cite_note-:6-9) It can only be used in low-moisture products like crackers, crisp cookies, choux pastries,[[1]](#cite_note-:1-1)[[6]](#cite_note-:8-6)[[9]](#cite_note-:6-9) where it results in a very fine, light, and tender crumb.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) In moist products like quickbreads, the ammonia will leave behind an unpleasantly bitter taste.[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2)[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) Because of its bench tolerance, baker's ammonia can be used in large-batch recipes.[[5]](#cite_note-:7-5)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

If you can't get your hands on baker's ammonia, try substituting in the same amount of baking powder.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7) Some recipes may require the addition of some baking soda as well.[[7]](#cite_note-:0-7)

## Recipes

[edit | edit source]

Recipes using baker's ammonia
